claim: The anomalously low entropy in the zodiac (3.7149 bits) and astronomical (3.7471 bits) sections relative to the text-only section (3.9016 bits) is produced by a label-encoding convention in which short positional labels (star names, month labels) are drawn from a restricted glyph sub-alphabet, reducing effective entropy rather than reflecting a different cipher.
evidence_for: Zodiac section has only 1,297 words across 12 folios â€” consistent with sparse label-heavy layout
Entropy gradient across sections (zodiac 3.71 < astronomical 3.75 < biological 3.80 < herbal 3.85 < text-only 3.90) tracks visually observed label density
Strong prior belief (0.704â€“0.726) that zodiac entropy reflects label-oriented encoding
evidence_against: If labels are simply shorter words, average word length in zodiac should be significantly below corpus mean of 5.03 â€” this needs verification
Some zodiac folios contain longer running text, not only labels, which would dilute the label-entropy effect
test: Segment zodiac and astronomical folios into 'label tokens' (words appearing beside illustrations, isolated by white space) versus 'running text tokens'; compute glyph entropy separately for each segment and compare to herbal and text-only section entropy.
test_metric: Glyph entropy of zodiac label tokens vs. zodiac running-text tokens vs. herbal section; mean word length of label tokens
test_threshold: Label-token entropy < 3.5 bits AND running-text entropy > 3.8 bits would confirm the label-encoding hypothesis; if both segments share entropy ~3.71, a different explanation is needed.

tests_run.details: Observed glyph entropy: 3.8627 bits. Expected for hypothesis: ~4.0 bits. Difference: 0.1373 bits.

reasoning: Isolating the entropy drop to label tokens would sharply constrain the cipher model for those sections, potentially revealing the restricted sub-alphabet used for labels.
